coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and structural evolution of hydrogenated amorphous silicon carbide thin film with carbon nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Mingming
Jiang, Lihua
Sun, Yihua
Xiao, Ting
Xiang, Peng
Tan, Xinyu
coredata.dc:description: A new hydrogenated amorphous silicon carbide (a-SiCx:H) thin film with carbon nanostructure is grown through plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) method. The structural evolution and growth mechanism of the as deposited a-SiCx:H thin film are explored and further discussed with different substrate temperatures. The results show that the structure of the a-SiCx:H thin film is a multiphase structure of carbon nanostructures embedded into CSiO(H) amorphous matrix, and the carbon nanostructure is a spheroidal graphite-like structure. With increasing substrate temperature, the carbon nanostructure gradually grows up through incorporation with surrounding nanostructures. When the substrate temperature increases further to 300 °C, the ultrafine basic structure units of graphite are found within the carbon nanostructure. In addition, the disorder of the thin film first increases and then decreases with increasing substrate temperature, which give rise to band tail states among the π-π⁎ band gap. The change of structure of as synthesized a-SiCx:H thin film with different substrate temperatures supports the sub-plantation model. Furthermore, a tunable optical band gap depended on structure of the carbon nanostructures is demonstrated at different substrate temperatures, which may bring about a potential of application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanostructures
PECVD
Substrate temperature
Sub-plantation model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of as the deposited a-SiCx:H thin film with different substrate temperatures; (a-e) Cross-sectional view and (f) top view. (The scale bar of (a-e) and (f) are 100 nm and 10 nm, respectively.)

(a) FTIR spectra of the as deposited a-SiCx:H thin film at different substrate temperatures; (b) the deconvolution of the CH n vibration band. (the colourful version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a-e) C1s peak of the samples deposited at different substrate temperatures; (f) the percent content of the sp2 CC, sp3 CC and CSi bonds obtained by decomposed XPS C1s peak. (the colourful version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Typical HR-TEM images. (a), (c) and (e) show HR-TEM images of the as deposited a-SiCx:H thin film at 100, 200 and 300 °C, respectively. (b), (d) and (f) show the partial enlarged views of (a), (c) and (e), respectively. The insets of (a), (c) and (e) show corresponding electron diffraction pattern. The inset of (f) shows a fast Fourier transform of atomic scale image. (the colourful version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Raman spectra of the samples deposited at different substrate temperatures; (b) the change of G peak FWHM, G peak position and ID/IG with substrate temperature. (the colourful version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram of the formation of the carbon nanostructures. (the colourful version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Optical band gaps of the film deposited at different substrate temperatures.

The percent content of carbon, silicon, oxygen in the films and the sp3 CC bond fraction, sp2 CC bond fraction and CSi bond fraction obtained by deconvolution of XPS C1s spectra for the films prepared at different substrate temperatures. (The atom content before ion-etching is in parentheses).

head.ce:title: Synthesis and structural evolution of hydrogenated amorphous silicon carbide thin film with carbon nanostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, China Three Gorges University, 8 Daxue Road, Yichang, Hubei 443002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Inorganic Nonmetallic Crystalline and Energy Conversion Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, China Three Gorges University, 8 Daxue Road, Yichang, Hubei 443002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new hydrogenated amorphous silicon carbide (a-SiCx:H) thin film with carbon nanostructure is grown through plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) method. The structural evolution and growth mechanism of the as deposited a-SiCx:H thin film are explored and further discussed with different substrate temperatures. The results show that the structure of the a-SiCx:H thin film is a multiphase structure of carbon nanostructures embedded into CSiO(H) amorphous matrix, and the carbon nanostructure is a spheroidal graphite-like structure. With increasing substrate temperature, the carbon nanostructure gradually grows up through incorporation with surrounding nanostructures. When the substrate temperature increases further to 300 °C, the ultrafine basic structure units of graphite are found within the carbon nanostructure. In addition, the disorder of the thin film first increases and then decreases with increasing substrate temperature, which give rise to band tail states among the π-π⁎ band gap. The change of structure of as synthesized a-SiCx:H thin film with different substrate temperatures supports the sub-plantation model. Furthermore, a tunable optical band gap depended on structure of the carbon nanostructures is demonstrated at different substrate temperatures, which may bring about a potential of application.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, multiple types of carbon nanostructures have been reported in literature, with most common ones being carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanotubes, carbon nanowires, carbon nanowalls, carbon nanorods and so on [1–4]. The formation of various nanostructures with different dimensionalities is believed to be related to the variable hybridization of carbon atomic orbital. Based on experimental and theoretical analysis, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of size less than 100 nm exhibit strong quantum confinement and edge effects [5]. Considering the diversity of carbon materials, the quantum confinement and edge effects should be common in carbon nanomaterials, only which may become more pronounced when their sizes are cut down to ca. 10 nm, and hence induce new properties. As a fascinating material, indeed, the zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures have numerous excellent properties such as large surface, excellent thermal/chemical stability, steady PL characteristics, high carrier transport mobility, up-conversion fluorescence emission characteristics, effectively tuneable optical band gap, etc., which results in their potential applications in various fields including the solar cells photocatalysis, sensor, LED and energy conversion/storage devices [6–10].

Hence, the development of carbon nanostructures has entered the high-speed track. Currently available carbon nanostructures are generally produced by typical physical and chemical routes from carbon-based organics including laser ablation [11–13], electrochemical oxidation [14], combustion [15], hydrothermal treatment [16,17] and ultrasonic or microwave methods [18–20]. A functional GQDs containing certain conjugated carbon atoms and oxygen-containing groups have been fabricated through electrochemical approach by Liangti Qu and co-author [21], which were demonstrated to be attractive as efficient electron acceptor and carrier transporting paveways in polymer photovoltaic cells. In the Jun Ke et al.'s report [22], up-conversion CQDs material as visible light responsive component has been synthesized by hydrothermal method for efficient enhancement of photocatalytic performance. However, the carbon nanostructures synthesized by these technologies are generally some of the colloid or solution, which limits their direct application in some photoelectric devices such as solar cells [23], LED [10,24], catalyst [12] etc. If there is a nanofilm material with carbon nanostructures, the significance of the carbon nanostructures in photoelectric field will be significantly enhanced. The film-based carbon nanostructures can be grown directly on substrate, which may be more convenient to be fixed or stored. In this context, it has become urgent to develop an effective technology for producing this nanofilm material. Yet, no any efficient chemical or physical methods have been reported so far for fabricating the nanofilm material with well-confined carbon nanostructures.

We have earlier synthesized a-SiCx:H thin films and situ‑silicon quantum dots in SiCx:H/a-C:H hetero-multilayer films using PECVD method [25,26]. In continuation to our work on different carbon nanostructures, herein, a carbon nanostructure of new type is grown in-situ in a-SiCx:H thin film by PECVD method. Experiments show that the carbon nanostructures are more like turbostratic carbon structure, and the size of the carbon nanostructures can be controlled by the deposited parameters. To further clarify the growth process of the carbon nanostructures in a-SiCx:H thin film, the structure and growth mechanism of the carbon nanostructure are explored and further discussed with different substrate temperatures. The results indicate that the structure and morphology of the carbon nanostructure can be strongly influenced by the substrate temperature, which is associated with growth mechanism of the a-SiCx:H thin film.

Various carbon nanostructure grown in-situ in a-SiCx:H thin film are synthesized by the PECVD process in a reactor consisting of two parallel electrodes, which energy be supplied by a radio-frequency power supply. In order to ensure the requirement of various characterization techniques, the sample is deposited simultaneously on the microscope glass slides, polished n-type crystalline silicon (100) and crystalline KBr substrates, and the cleaning process of substrate has been described in previous publication [27]. A gaseous mixture of silane (SiH4) and hydrogen (H2) at a volume ratio of 1: 9 is used as silicon source, and the pure methane (CH4 purity 99.995%) is used as carbon source. The gaseous mixture and pure methane flow rate are fixed at 7 and 14 stand-state cubic centimeter per minute (sccm), respectively. A pressure of 60 Pa is maintained in the chamber during the deposition with radio frequency power for plasma set at 120 W. In order to investigate accurately the influence of substrate temperature on structure properties of the film, the substrate temperature varies from 100 to 300 °C (the step is 50 °C). The deposition time is maintained at about 40 min to obtain the appropriate film thickness.

The thickness and surface topography of the film on crystalline silicon substrate are firstly examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The characteristic of chemical bonds of the as deposited film on crystalline KBr substrate is observed by a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (NEXUS, Thermo Nicolet Corporation, Madison, Wisconsin) in the range of 500–3500 cm−1 with a resolution of 2 cm−1. The morphological and structural evolution of the film with substrate temperature are studied by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and Dispersive Raman Microscope (Senterra R200-L) with 532 nm excitation. During the TEM sample preparation, the a-SiCx:H thin film is separated from the KBr substrate by soaking in deionized water, and then the sheet is transferred to a standard TEM grid, and go through dry finally. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizes the chemical composition and carbon hybridization structure on the surface and the inside of the films by collecting the data of before and after 30 s ion-etching. XPS measurement is carried out by an X-ray spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher EscaLab 250Xi) using a monochromatic Al K α source (E photon = 1486.6 eV). The XPS wide scan spectra are acquired in the range of 0–1350 eV at a step of 1.0 eV, using a 200 eV pass energy. The C1s and Si2p narrow scan spectra are acquired at 0.1 eV step with a pass energy of 50 eV. To study the influence of the structure on optical property, the Tauc optical band gap is calculated from the curve of (αhν)1/2vs.hν.

To prove the structure of the a-SiCx:H thin film with carbon nanostructures, the SEM image of the as deposited a-SiCx:H thin film is surveyed firstly as shown in Fig. 1 . The top views of the thin films deposited at different substrate temperatures show that the thin films are smooth and scratch resistant as Fig. 1(f) shows surface topography of the sample deposited at 100 °C [28]. Cross-sectional views show that there are some straight borders and the uniform thickness. As the substrate temperature increases from 100 to 300 °C, the border of the thin films become indistinct and the thickness of the film changes in the range of 311–476 nm, which may be associated with sub-plantation model mentioned below.

Fig. 2(a) presents the FTIR absorption spectra of as deposited a-SiCx:H thin film after normalized by the film thickness. There are five obvious absorption peaks at around 795, 1020, 1250, 1650, 2130 cm−1 and one obvious absorption band at around 2800–3000 cm−1, which correspond to SiC bond stretching vibration, SiC-H/Si-O-Si bond vibration, SiCH3 bending vibration, CC vibration, CSi-H vibration and CH n vibration mode, respectively [29–31]. To determine the structure of CH n chemical bond, the CH n vibration band in the range of 2700–3100 cm−1 is decomposed into three vibration modes about 2875, 2923 and 2960 cm−1, which are assigned to the vibration of the symmetrical CH3, CH and asymmetrical CH3, respectively as shown in Fig. 2(b) [32]. In this CH n band, the CH vibration peak first decreases with increasing substrate temperature from 100 to 200 °C, and then the CH3 vibration peaks decrease obviously with increasing substrate temperature from 200 to 300 °C. Strong SiC and SiCH peaks suggest that SiC bond is a main phase in the film. In the a-SiCx:H thin film, Si atom will bond with sp3 C atom and further form SiC random network. Weak CSiH peak and strong CH n band can be attributed to the much more carbon than silicon in the film as the XPS study below. The SiOSi peak implies the introduction of oxygen atom in the film, and the similar reports have been shown in [33]. The oxygen contamination can be attributed to the influence of background H2O due to the use of hygroscopic KBr substrate [33,34]. In this case, the H2O is carried into the plasma chamber through hygroscopic KBr substrate. After a series of chemical reactions, the H2O is decomposed into radical of OH, and further gets into the films. In addition, the detection of CC/CC bond in a-SiCx:H thin film is generally very difficult, however, conspicuous CC peak suggests a mass of sp2 carbon phase in the thin films.

XPS is a direct and quantitative method for the characterization of carbon hybridization structure. In order to understand the microstructure of the a-SiCx:H thin film, XPS on the surface and the inside of the films are studied. Two strong peaks of Si2p (101 eV), C1s (284.5 eV) and a weak O1s peak (532 eV) are observed in all the samples. Considering the relative sensitivity factor, the carbon, silicon and oxygen contents are calculated from the ratio of the relative element peak intensity to the total intensity in the XPS [6,24]. The results are shown in Table 1 . As substrate temperature increases, the silicon content first increases slightly and then decreases slightly. A small quantity of oxygen content seems to be unchanging with increasing substrate temperature and there is no significant different in the oxygen content of before and after ion-etching, which has been attributed the influence of background H2O during the deposition. According to the typical fittings for carbon nanotube [35], CQDs [6,20] and amorphous silicon carbide films [36], chemical bond of present C1s peak can be deconvoluted into three carbon moieties: sp2 CC (284.6 eV), sp3 CC (285.2 eV) and CSi (283.5 eV) with constant full width at half maximum (FWHM, set at 1.5 eV). Fig. 3(a–e) show present C1s peak of XPS spectra with different substrate temperatures after the best Gaussian fit. It is obvious that the sp2 CC bond fraction first decreases and then increases with increasing substrate temperature, which is completely opposite with the change of sp3 CC bond as shown in Fig. 3(f). The fraction of CSi bond firstly increases and then decreases, which is agree with the Si atom content and SiC peak intensity of FTIR spectra. Vast sp2 CC bonds imply that a graphite phase is synthesized, which may support the structural model with the carbon nanostructures embedded into CSiO(H) amorphous matrix [37].

In order to study the morphological and structural evolution of as deposited a-SiCx:H thin film with substrate temperature, the three samples of substrate temperature at 100, 200 and 300 °C are selected to observe the typical plan-view HR-TEM image, as shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a), (c) and (e) show HR-TEM images of the carbon nanostructures within the a-SiCx:H thin films deposited at 100, 200 and 300 °C, respectively. For the sample deposited at 100 °C, it is obvious that a lot of spheroidal carbon nanostructures (around 10–20 nm in diameter) gather into a mass. For the sample deposited at 200 °C, the size of the carbon nanostructures (around 10–40 nm in diameter) are larger than that of the carbon nanostructures deposited at 100 °C. When the substrate temperature further increases to 300 °C, the size of the carbon nanostructures (around 20–80 nm in diameter) increases continuously. As the size increases, the boundary of carbon nanostructures becomes clearer. The insets of Fig. 4(a), (c) and (e) show corresponding selected area electron diffraction pattern in the form of Debye rings with diffuse background. Such electron diffraction rings elucidate the presence of nanocrystal in the amorphous matrix [38]. To further investigate the structure of the carbon nanostructures, the partial enlarged views of Fig. 4(a), (c) and (e) are shown in Fig. 4(b), (d) and (f), respectively. In the Fig. 4(b), it can be found that three adjacent nanostructures (labeled as 1, 2, 3) are growing through spreading out layer by layer. The average interlayer spacing of the nanostructures is about 4.1 Å measured by the software of DigitalMicrograph 3.7. As the substrate temperature increases to 200 °C, the nanostructures have a noteworthy enlargement which can be attributed to the merging of the nanostructures as shown in Fig. 4(d). There the two nanostructures are gradually melting to form a large nanostructure. The average interlayer spacing of the nanostructures decreases to 3.9 Å. When the substrate temperature further increases to 300 °C, as mentioned above, the nanostructures are larger. Furthermore, ultrafine basic structure units of graphite are observed embedded in nanostructures as shown in Fig. 4(f). The size of the basic structure units is about 3–5 nm, and the interplanar spacing is about 2.7 nm. Although revealing every individual nanocrystal in the recorded atomic scale image is difficult in real space, the corresponding reciprocal space pattern is calculated as inset in Fig. 4(f). Two lattice planes (002 and 101) of sp2 graphite carbon are marked in the calculated reciprocal space pattern of atomic-scale resolved image by the software. So, basic structure unit of graphite can be confirmed, and the interplanar spacing of 2.7 nm may belong to (020) of graphite [39].

To again confirm the carbon nanostructures, the Raman spectra with 532 nm excitation are surveyed as shown in Fig. 5(a). A D band at around 1360 cm−1 and a G band at around 1590 cm−1 are observed in all the samples with different substrate temperatures. Strong G peak signifies the presence of graphite-like sp2 CC phase in the thin films, and obvious D peak is due to the limitation of graphite domain size induced by grain boundaries or imperfections like stacking faults [40]. For graphene samples G peak is generally presented at 1580 cm−1 while graphene oxide has G peak upshifted to 1594 cm−1 due to presence of oxygen [2]. For the present samples, the position of G peak at 1590 cm−1 signifies that the material is graphitic in nature with presence of some amount of oxygen. According to the studies about GQDs, oxygen-containing functional groups including COC, OH and CO/COOH are introduced at the edge and on the basal plane after oxidation, which results to their (002) interlayer spacing (3.43 Å) is larger than that of bulk graphite (3.34 Å) [41]. Small amounts of oxygen and silicon atoms are detected in present sample as shown in FTIR and XPS survey results thereinbefore. And considering the Si atom radius is larger than that of carbon, the interlayer spacing (about 4.1/3.9 Å) of the carbon nanostructures is larger than bulk graphite even GQDs that can be attributed to the incorporation of Si and O atoms into the carbon nanostructures.

It is well known that the G peak position and the intensity ratio of D peak and G peak are related to the structure of the carbon nanostructures. Additionally, the intensity ratio is strictly connected to the presence of six-fold aromatic ring and correlated to the size of sp2 carbon nanostructures. The position and FWHM of G peak are related to the sp2 carbon and sp3 carbon content, respectively [32]. For present samples, the changes of the position and FWHM of G peak are shown in Fig. 5(b). With increasing substrate temperature, the G peak position first upward shifts and then downward shifts when the FWHM of G peak first increases and then decreases. The changes of the position and FWHM of G peak reveal that the degree of disorder first increases and then decreases with increasing substrate temperature [28,42]. Meanwhile, the ID/IG ratio first decreases and then increases, which indicates that the number of six-numbered rings of the nanostructures first increases and then decreases with substrate temperature. The increasing amount of six-numbered rings in the nanostructures can be attributed to the extension of graphene layer by growing up of nanostructures. The results of Raman well correspond to those of FTIR spectra, XPS and HR-TEM.

From the above analysis, the a-SiCx:H thin film with carbon nanostructures is successfully synthesized by PECVD system. The date and analysis show that the films are a multiphase structure with carbon nanostructures. With different substrate temperatures different morphological nanostructures exist in the films as shown in Schematic diagram of Fig. 6 . At low substrate temperature (100 °C), many small carbon nanostructures are gathered together and grown through spreading out of graphene layer. With the increase of substrate temperature to 200 °C, the size of the carbon nanostructures gradually increases to 10–40 nm from 10 to 20 nm through incorporation with surrounding nanostructures. When the substrate temperature further increases to 300 °C, the carbon nanostructures surrounded by graphene layers are replaced by the structure of larger nanostructures embedded with ultrafine basic structure units of graphite. With the introduction of Si and O atoms in the carbon nanostructures, they tend to connect with the edge and graphene basal plane which leads to a wider interlayer spacing (about 4.1/3.9 Å) of than that of bulk graphite (3.34 Å) and GQDs (3.43 Å). Refer to some studies about influence of the substrate temperature on amorphous carbon films [43], the substrate temperature determines the mobility of the depositing particles in deposited process. According to sub-plantation model [44,45], energetic ions and neutrals can penetrate the surface entering subsurface interstitial sites. The ion implantation is thought to be closely associated with a local density and compressive stress. At a low substrate temperature, the energetic ions and neutrals directly come to rest after implantation into the surface atom interstitial sites. As a result, the small size of the nanostructures with more sp2 CC bonds can form. With the increase of substrate temperature, the energetic ions and neutrals can be implanted into the deep of the film which causes an increasing local density and in turn leads to increase of sp3 CC bond content. As the substrate temperature increases further, the sp3 CC bond content decreases, which may be due to the extremely high energy of ions caused by the substrate temperature. Because the thermalization and relaxation stages allow the excess density to relax to zero, and cause a loss of sp3 CC bonding at higher ion energies [44]. Moreover, the higher mobility of the particles brings about the larger size of the carbon nanostructures.

The effect of substrate temperature on the structure of the a-SiCx:H thin film has been discussed above, and the next step is to study the optical properties of this structure. Fig. 7 shows optical band gaps of the a-SiCx:H films deposited at different substrate temperatures. It can be observed that the optical band gap first decreases and then increases with increasing substrate temperature. The change of optical band gap can be explained by the band tail states and the quantum confinement effect related with carbon nanostructures. On the one hand, according to the quantum confinement effect, a larger size of the carbon nanostructures generally causes a narrower optical band gap [46–48]. On the other hand, in carbon materials, the optical band gap generally can be associated with the band tail states of the π and π⁎ states. The narrowing of the optical band gap with increasing substrate temperature may result from the formation of band tail states among the π-π⁎ band gap. As mentioned above, with increase of substrate temperature from 100 °C to 300 °C, the band tail states controlled by disorder first increases and then decreases. The band tail states reach deep into the gap and form narrower optical band gap when substrate temperature equals to 200 °C.

In summary, a new a-SiCx:H thin films with carbon nanostructures have been synthesized from hydrogen diluted silane and methane mixtures by PECVD method. The data of FTIR, XPS, HR-TEM and Raman show that the structure of the as deposited a-SiCx:H thin film is a multiphase structure of carbon nanostructures embedded into a-SiCx:H thin film, and the carbon nanostructure is a spheroidal graphite-like structure. The formation mechanism of the carbon nanostructures is speculated by observing the effect of the substrate temperature. Firstly, with increasing substrate temperature, the size of nanostructures gradually increases through incorporation with surrounding nanostructures, and the nanostructures surrounded by graphene layers are replaced by the structure of more large nanostructures embedded with ultrafine basic structure units of graphite when the substrate temperature increases to 300 °C. In addition, with the increase of substrate temperature from 100 to 300 °C, the sp3 carbon content first increases and then decreases. The increase of sp3 carbon content in the a-SiCx:H thin films causes the larger disorder in carbon nanostructure, which give rise to the large band tail states among the band gap. The change of structure of as synthesized carbon nanostructures with different substrate temperatures supports the sub-plantation model. Finally, the change of structure is revealed by optical property of the carbon nanostructures, so the optical property can commendably tune by controlling substrate temperature.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61604087 and U1765105), the State Scholarship fund of the China Scholarship Council (CSC201708420285) and the Research Fund for Excellent Dissertation of China Three Gorges University (2018SSPY058).
